title: Snickerdoodles
subtitle: Soft American sugar biscuits rolled in cinnamon sugar
excerpt: A simple American biscuit made with cream of tartar for a slight tang and a soft, puffy centre, rolled in cinnamon sugar before baking. They come out of the oven domed and set flat as they cool.
sourceNotes: Snickerdoodles are an American biscuit (cookie) whose name and recipe appear in New England domestic cookery from the late nineteenth century. The Joy of Cooking (Irma Rombauer, 1931) includes the recipe. The defining characteristics (cream of tartar for a slight acidic tang and the cinnamon-sugar rolling) are consistent across documented recipes from the 1880s onward. The cream of tartar reacts with bicarbonate to produce a puffier, more tender result than baking powder alone.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Thaw at room temperature. Or freeze the rolled dough balls before rolling in cinnamon sugar; bake from frozen, adding 2 minutes to bake time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dough balls can be refrigerated overnight before rolling in cinnamon sugar and baking.

content.text: Snickerdoodles use cream of tartar rather than baking powder as the leavener (combined with bicarbonate of soda). The cream of tartar provides both acid for the rise and a subtle tang that distinguishes them from a plain sugar biscuit. The dough is soft and slightly sticky; chilling it for 30 minutes makes rolling it into balls much easier. They bake puffed and dome-shaped and flatten slightly as they cool.

attrs.prepNote: divided: 250 g for the dough, 50 g for rolling

content.text: Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{cream-of-tartar}}, and {{fine-salt}}. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and 250 g of {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes. Beat in the {{whole-eggs}} one at a time. Add the flour mixture and stir until just combined. Chill the dough for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190°C (170°C fan). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Mix the remaining 50 g {{caster-sugar}} with the {{ground-cinnamon}} in a small bowl. Roll tablespoons of dough into balls and roll each ball in the cinnamon sugar to coat. Place on the lined trays, spacing them 5 cm apart. Bake for 10-12 minutes until the edges are set and the centres look barely done — they will firm as they cool. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack.
